title: From Evolution to Therapy: Precision Targeting of Functionally Divergent Integrins
keywords: sequence collection (27 vertebrate taxa; 4 paralogs; 2 outgroups)
phylogeny inference and divergence time estimation
structural mapping onto αiib–β3 binding pocket

summary.hook: This poster proposes an evolutionary framework to improve selectivity in integrin-targeted therapies by distinguishing highly conserved RGD-binding cores from lineage-derived, paralog-specific regions within the PS2 α-integrin group (αIIb, α5, αV, α8).
summary.summary_30s: This poster proposes an evolutionary framework to improve selectivity in integrin-targeted therapies by distinguishing highly conserved RGD-binding cores from lineage-derived, paralog-specific regions within the PS2 α-integrin group (αIIb, α5, αV, α8). Using orthologs from 27 vertebrate species spanning about 450 million years, the authors reconstruct a time-calibrated phylogeny, infer ancestral sequences, and map conservation/divergence signals onto structural/functional domains (including the αIIb–β3 ligand-binding pocket). They conclude that many adverse effects (e.g., bleeding) may arise from drugs binding conserved interfaces shared across paralogs, and that targeting αIIb-specific derived regions could improve selectivity while preserving necessary shared functions.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Explore whether evolutionary patterns of conservation/divergence across PS2 α-integrin paralogs can identify functionally important and subtype-specific regions relevant for selective therapeutic targeting.

Approach: Population: Orthologs of PS2 α-integrins (ITGA subunits αIIb, α5, αV, α8) from 27 representative vertebrate species. Primary outcome: Identification of conserved vs lineage-specific residues/regions; inferred ancestral functions; implications for selectivity targeting.
Finding: PS2 integrins originated before vertebrate evolution; four paralogs diverged through stepwise duplication during early vertebrate evolution.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Integrin-directed therapy failures (bleeding, limited selectivity, compensatory signaling, context-dependent effects) are likely linked to targeting conserved RGD-binding interfaces shared across PS2 paralogs; precision targeting should instead prioritize αIIb-specific lineage-derived regions to reduce bleeding risk while improving selectivity. Implications (poster-grounded): Evolutionary constraint metrics may help prioritize safer, more selective drug targets within integrin families. Structural mapping of lineage-specific substitutions can identify candidate regions for subtype-selective targeting strategies. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Compensatory signaling among paralogs likely arises from conserved regions inherited from an ancestral broadly expressed RGD-binding receptor; off-target effects arise from non-selective binding to conserved interfaces. Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): References include Campbell et al.
key_numbers: 27 vertebrate species; 4 paralogs; 2 outgroups (listed in Methods box).
\~450 million years of vertebrate evolutionary history (described in introduction). Divergence times displayed in Figure 1 as Mya.
Orthologs of PS2 α-integrins (ITGA subunits αIIb, α5, αV, α8) from 27 representative vertebrate species.

takeaways: PS2 integrins originated before vertebrate evolution; four paralogs diverged through stepwise duplication during early vertebrate evolution.
The MRCA of PS2 integrins likely functioned as a broadly expressed RGD-binding ECM receptor; conserved regions may explain compensatory signaling among paralogs.
The platelet-restricted fibrinogen-binding function of αIIb is a derived innovation linked to vertebrate hemostasis.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What alignment and phylogenetic inference methods were used (software, substitution models, support metrics)?

what_to_ask.question: What are the key duplication events you infer in early vertebrate evolution, and where do they appear on the time-calibrated phylogeny?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How sensitive is the phylogeny to alignment choices and domain trimming, especially in fast-evolving loops?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: What would an “αIIb-specific, lineage-derived” drug concept look like (orthosteric vs allosteric vs antibody epitope)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
